title: Company of One
author: Paul Jarvis
subtitle: Why Staying Small Is the Next Big Thing for Business
description: A counterintuitive business book that argues growth is not always the goal. Paul Jarvis makes the case for staying small, focused, and profitable — building a business that serves your life rather than consuming it. Through case studies of 'companies of one' — freelancers, small agency owners, and solopreneurs — Jarvis shows that with the right approach, you can build a sustainable, profitable business without venture capital, employees, or the pressure to scale.
keyIdeas: Growth is a choice — not every business needs to scale
Profitability is more important than growth or valuation
Saying no to clients and opportunities protects your focus
Systems and automation reduce the need for employees
A Company of One prioritizes resilience over rapid expansion
Direct relationships with customers are a competitive advantage
Simplicity in product and process reduces overhead and stress
Work should fit around your life, not the other way around
Pricing should reflect value, not market rates or cost-plus
Continuous learning and adaptation replace the need for a large team
keyTakeaways: You can build a profitable, sustainable business without ever raising VC money or scaling
Growth is not the default goal — question whether you actually need to grow
Profitability first: a profitable small business is more resilient than a high-growth, unprofitable one
Saying no to the wrong clients and opportunities is as important as saying yes to the right ones
Systems and automation let you scale output without scaling headcount
Direct customer relationships build loyalty that no marketing budget can buy
Simplicity in products, services, and operations reduces stress and increases quality
Pricing based on value, not cost or competition, increases both revenue and satisfaction
Work should serve your life — if your business owns your time, you have built a cage
Resilience comes from adaptability, not size
whoShouldRead: Freelancers and solopreneurs building sustainable independent businesses
Small agency owners questioning whether they want to scale
Anyone tired of the 'grow at all costs' startup culture
Side hustlers turning their passion into a business without quitting their day job

relatedBooks.title: The Hard Thing About Hard Things
